one_line_takeaway: An open RL-with-reasoning recipe (lightweight warmstart SFT + harmful/harmless/ambiguous prompt mixing + split safety/helpfulness rewards) lets a 1.5B model reason per-prompt about safety and beat much larger jailbreak defenses on the safety-refusal trade-off.
key_finding: TARS achieves the best safety-refusal Pareto frontier. A 1.5B TARS model attains a better safety-refusal trade-off than 7-8B baselines (Llama-RR, Mistral-RR circuit breakers, Llama-8B, SafeChain) and beats 8B circuit breakers with 6.6x fewer parameters; the 7B TARS outperforms all. In the controlled study, both RL AND reasoning are needed: RL-without-reasoning beats SFT-with-reasoning, but TARS (RL+reasoning) beats both, plus DPO. TARS shows adaptive compute allocation, reasoning longest on ambiguous 'Unqualified Advice' Sorry-Bench prompts and shortest on clear 'Hate Speech' prompts. Representationally, TARS separates GCG-attack vs ambiguous prompts best (soft-SVM margin 2.21 vs SFT 1.03, DPO 1.45, RL 0.88) even before the reasoning block. Under attack, when reasoning is properly formatted (EOT present) TARS DSR is 92.79% vs SFT 82.73% under GCG and 94.45% vs 72.97% under PAIR; TARS also stays safe even when GCG breaks the format (83.82% vs 78.90%), sometimes emitting a harmful string in place of reasoning but a safe final answer after EOT.
core_contribution: TARS (Training Adaptive Reasoners for Safety) is an open, reproducible RL recipe that post-trains an LLM to reason (long chain-of-thought inside <think>...</think>) about the safety of a request on a per-prompt basis before answering. The paper's specific contribution is not the idea of reasoning-for-safety (which prior SFT/DPO work and concurrent proprietary work like Deliberative Alignment explored), but a systematic, first-principles identification of three design choices that make RL-based safety reasoning actually work: (1) a deliberately 'lightweight' warmstart SFT stage (low LR, early stopping) that preserves generation diversity for RL exploration, (2) mixing harmful, harmless, and ambiguous prompts during RL to prevent the shortcut of refusing everything, and (3) a split reward design (a safety reward for harmful prompts, a separate task-completion/helpfulness reward for harmless prompts) rather than a single preference reward. The authors also contribute a controlled comparison isolating whether gains come from RL, from reasoning, or both, and an analysis of how jailbreak attacks manifest differently against reasoning models.
threat_model: Adversary submits harmful requests or jailbreak prompts to an aligned LLM to elicit harmful completions, while the model must remain compliant (non-over-refusing) on benign and ambiguously-worded-but-benign prompts. The defense must hold under both white-box attacks with access to model weights (GCG, AutoDAN) and black-box rhetorical/query attacks (PAIR, PAP). A specific new attack surface studied: because reasoning models have a dedicated CoT segment before the answer, GCG is adapted to target the generation right after the begin-of-thinking token to hijack the reasoning itself.
limitations: Small model scale (1.5B and 7B only); no test beyond 7B. Relies on curating prompts from existing datasets and distilling static R1 traces rather than collecting oracle safety reasoning traces, which the authors note could do better. Attacks are given fixed compute; the authors explicitly acknowledge GCG could be strengthened (more compute, better target strings, or targeting the reasoning trace directly) and expect such adaptive attacks to also degrade the defense. The Deliberative Alignment baseline is the authors' own replication (proprietary details undisclosed), so that comparison is approximate. Safety reward depends on the OpenAI Moderation API and GRM reward model, inheriting their blind spots. Evaluation is single-turn only, so multi-turn attacks (Crescendo, X-Teaming) are named but not benchmarked. Ambiguous training prompts (OR-Bench) differ in distribution from the XSTest/WildChat compliance evaluations.

datasets_used: Aegis AI Content Safety Dataset 2.0 (harmful prompts, SFT and RL)
Rainbow Teaming (adversarial harmful prompts generated against the SFT model, RL)
HarmBench (400 harmful behaviors + Llama-13B classifier, safety eval)
quantitative_results: 1.5B TARS beats 8B circuit-breaker defenses with 6.6x fewer parameters on the safety-refusal trade-off
Internal representation soft-SVM margins (GCG vs ambiguous prompts): TARS 2.21, DPO 1.45, SFT 1.03, RL 0.88
GCG, DSR when reasoning properly formatted (EOT present): TARS 92.79% vs SFT 82.73%
PAIR, DSR when reasoning properly formatted: TARS 94.45% vs SFT 72.97%
Percentage of responses with EOT token under GCG: TARS 68.65% vs SFT 91.85%; under PAIR: TARS 99.89% vs SFT 94.79%
DSR when format is broken (no EOT): TARS 83.82% vs SFT 78.90%
Sorry-Bench avg reasoning/answer token lengths (lambda=0.5 TARS): Hate Speech 289.88/165.18; Assistance with Crimes 306.01/249.07; Inappropriate Topics 371.67/316.39; Unqualified Advice 456.66/608.88
SFT dataset ~3600 (x,z,y) triplets from 1000 harmful prompts x 4 R1 traces; RL prompt pool = 2000 (e.g. lambda=0.7 -> 1400 harmful / 600 harmless+ambiguous)
Training cost: 4x A6000 GPUs, 5-10 hours per model
baselines_compared: Controlled internal comparisons from the same pi_SFT checkpoint: SFT-with-reasoning, DPO (RPO), RL-without-reasoning (RLHF)
